interactions.summary: Jiang finishes the Inquisition answer by explaining precision as a way to punish only the suspected Cathars and threaten the rest of the community; he then acknowledges classroom questions before moving to scapegoating.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answering an implied classroom question; student voices are mostly not captured.

interactions.summary: Jiang defines scapegoating through the ritual goat and then applies it to feudal antisemitism: Jews are used as sacrificial middlemen so peasants discharge anger and return to work.
He closes by defining religion as culture, worldview, and belief system: medieval Christians act from afterlife belief just as modern Chinese students act from education-as-life-success belief.

speaker_notes.note: Several student questions are implied by Jiang's acknowledgments, but the student words are not captured.

claims.claim: Inquisition precision is described as more effective than indiscriminate crusade violence because it identifies a minority, punishes them publicly, and makes resistance feel futile.
Jiang defines scapegoating as blaming someone else for one's troubles or distracting people from the real source of conflict.
The feudal scapegoat mechanism lets nobles preserve property and labor by allowing periodic violence against Jews, after which peasants return to work.
claims.temporal_scope: Medieval social-control model in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang defines religion broadly as culture, worldview, and belief system that drives action, whether medieval afterlife belief or modern education belief in China.
The lecture closes by saying the Crusades material is needed for future classes, especially the Renaissance and Protestant Reformation.

signature_moments.moment: The Inquisition wins by making resistance feel futile: they will figure out the truth, so people stop resisting.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the Inquisition into a psychological control system, not just a punishment system.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang describes anti-Jewish violence as a ritual sacrifice that cleanses peasant anger so the feudal economy can continue.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a ritual sacrifice of certain Jews in order to cleanse the peasants of their anger
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture's most disturbing and precise account of political scapegoating.

signature_moments.moment: Religion is not just church doctrine; it is any belief system strong enough to drive behavior, even exam culture.
signature_moments.source_phrase: That's a religion. That's a belief system
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It expands the episode's medieval argument into Jiang's broader definition of culture as lived faith.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Blaming or sacrificing another group to redirect social anger away from the real conflict.
Jiang's broad definition of religion as culture and belief system that organizes action.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang places this lecture immediately before the Renaissance and Protestant Reformation in the course sequence.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript repeatedly renders 'scapegoating' as 'skateboarding' or 'skip voting'; semantic extraction follows context and uses scapegoating.
